coredata.dc:title: Multicolor nitrogen dots for rapid detection of thiram and chlorpyrifos in fruit and vegetable samples
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Zhijiao
Chen, Zhengyi
Li, Gongke
Hu, Yuling
coredata.dc:description: The development of sensitive fluorescence sensors and efficient preparation of samples is a challenge in the detection of pesticides in complex samples. In this study, multicolor nitrogen dots (M-Ndots) were synthesised via microwave irradiation at 140 °C for 10 min with 5-amino-1H-tetrazole and p-phenylenediamine as precursors, which have a high fluorescence quantum yield of up to 31%. Furthermore, the M-Ndots were employed as fluorescence sensors for pesticide detection by being combined with a gas membrane separation device, to eliminate the interference from the complex sample matrix. In this process, the M-Ndots were used for sensing thiram and chlorpyrifos through their affinities to Cu2+ and Fe3+, respectively. Because thiram could decompose into volatile CS2, its derivate was sensed using the fluorescence of M-Ndots via a complexation reaction with Cu2+. Chlorpyrifos, due to its volatility, can reduce the Fe3+ ion by inhibiting the activity of acetylcholinesterase, which produces H2O2 to oxidise Fe2+. In a real application, the time consumption for 96 samples was less than 30 min in one run of the gas membrane separation device. The recoveries for thiram and chlorpyrifos ranged from 90.0% to 115.0%, and the analytical results were validated using LC-MS/MS methods, with relative errors ranging from −7.4% to 10.1%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multicolor nitrogen dots
Miniaturized array gas membrane separation
Thiram
Chlorpyrifos
Fluorescence rapid detection
Fruit and vegetable

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Thiram detection in fruits based on miniaturized array gas membrane separation/M-Ndots sensing
Chlorpyrifos detection in vegetables based on miniaturized array gas membrane separation/M-Ndots sensing
Design of the sensing strategy of M-Ndots for pesticide detection
The application of M-Ndots in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FL spectra of the M-Ndots; and (b) CIE chromaticity coordinates of the M-Ndots.
(a) TEM image, (b) AFM image, (c) XPS survey and (d) deconvoluted N1s XPS spectra of the M-Ndots.
Schematic illustration of detection principle based on the M-Ndots and metal ions (a-i:control, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Fe3+).
The scheme of sensing thiram (a) and chlorpyrifos (b) with M-Ndots.

Schematic illustration of (a) thiram and (b) chlorpyrifos detection principle based on the M-Ndots. 1. Fluorescence spectra of M-Ndots; 2. Fluorescence “off” by Cu2+; 3. Fluorescence “on” in the present of thiram. (b) Fluorescence spectra (λex = 320 nm) of the nanoprobe upon addition of increasing concentrations of thiram (0.0–2.5 μg/mL). Inset: Linear relationship between F/F0 and the concentrations of thiram (0.5–2.5 μg/mL). (c) Schematic illustration of chlorpyrifos detection principle based on the M-Ndots. 1. Fluorescence spectra of M-Ndots; 2. Fluorescence “off” by Fe3+ which from the Fe2+ oxidation in the present of Ach, AchE and ChOx; 3. Fluorescence “on” after the chlorpyrifos inhibited AchE. (d) Fluorescence spectra (λex = 360 nm) of the nanoprobe upon addition of increasing logarithmic concentrations of chlorpyrifos (0.00–0.50 μg/mL). Inset: Linear relationship between F/F0 and the concentrations of chlorpyrifos (0.01–0.50 μg/mL). Error bars were obtained from three parallel experiments. The slit widths of emission and excitation were both 5 nm.
The determination of thiram and chlorpyrifos in fruit and vegetable samples based on M-Ndots coupled with gas membrane separation.
Recoveries for rapid detection of thiram (dithiocarbamate fungicides) and chlorpyrifos (Organophosphorus pesticide) based on M-Ndots coupled with gas membrane separation in real samples (n = 3)∗.
The comparison of M-Ndots to other quantum dots applied for fluorescence detection of pesticides.

row.entry: Formation of hydrogen bonding between carbon dots and atrazine
FRET between carbon dots and glyphosate
Using Enzymes (AChE); FRET between Q-CDs and TNB−
Gas membrane separation; Using Enzymes (AChE); Derivatization

head.ce:title: Multicolor nitrogen dots for rapid detection of thiram and chlorpyrifos in fruit and vegetable samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou 510275, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou 510275, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Tang and Chen contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of sensitive fluorescence sensors and efficient preparation of samples is a challenge in the detection of pesticides in complex samples. In this study, multicolor nitrogen dots (M-Ndots) were synthesised via microwave irradiation at 140 °C for 10 min with 5-amino-1H-tetrazole and p-phenylenediamine as precursors, which have a high fluorescence quantum yield of up to 31%. Furthermore, the M-Ndots were employed as fluorescence sensors for pesticide detection by being combined with a gas membrane separation device, to eliminate the interference from the complex sample matrix. In this process, the M-Ndots were used for sensing thiram and chlorpyrifos through their affinities to Cu2+ and Fe3+, respectively. Because thiram could decompose into volatile CS2, its derivate was sensed using the fluorescence of M-Ndots via a complexation reaction with Cu2+. Chlorpyrifos, due to its volatility, can reduce the Fe3+ ion by inhibiting the activity of acetylcholinesterase, which produces H2O2 to oxidise Fe2+. In a real application, the time consumption for 96 samples was less than 30 min in one run of the gas membrane separation device. The recoveries for thiram and chlorpyrifos ranged from 90.0% to 115.0%, and the analytical results were validated using LC-MS/MS methods, with relative errors ranging from −7.4% to 10.1%.

ce:section.ce:para: Dithiocarbamates and organophosphorus pesticides, such as thiram and chlorpyrifos, have been widely used to prevent fungal diseases [1] or to eliminate pests in agriculture for many years [2]. However, the residue from pesticide, even at trace levels, not only has potentially adverse health effects, but can also severely breakdown the ecosystem [3]. Consequently, the potential for their toxicological risk to human health is strictly regulated [4], and accurate quantification of such pesticides has become an urgent requirement [5]. Current detection methods for thiram or chlorpyrifos include high performance liquid chromatography/gas chromatography HPLC/GC instruments equipped with various detectors [6], surface-enhanced Raman scattering spectroscopy (SERS) [7] and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [8]. In comparison to these classical methods, quantum-dot fluorescence sensors are attractive because of their rapid, sensitive, and selective detection without sophisticated instrumentation. For example, Lin et al. developed carbon dots from wastepaper which they directly employed as a turn-on fluorescence probe to enable the detection of chlorpyrifos [9]. As the core of the fluorescence probe, the performance of carbon dots is largely dependent on the functional groups on their surface [10]. Zhang et al. used amine-terminated carbon dots as fluorescence probes for Cu2+ sensing by photo-induced electron transfer; this allowed quantitative detection methods for thiram to be built via the inner filter effect [11]. Therefore, the development of novel quantum dots for use in the detection of pesticides is of great interest.

Nitrogen-rich quantum dots (N-dots), make up a new member of quantum-dot family. They have garnered tremendous attention owing to their outstanding electronic and photonic properties [12]. These N-dots contain a higher percentage of electron-rich N atoms compared to most of the neighbouring carbon dots, which can drastically alter their electronic characteristics and exhibit unique luminescence properties [12–14]. Thus far, several researchers have successfully developed methods to prepare N-dots, in order to further investigated their use in biocompatible staining, imaging of amino-acids, determination of toxic cations, among other applications [15–18]. Although many reports have presented the excitation-dependence of N-dots, the shifts in their fluorescence (FL) emission wavelength are often accompanied by strong reductions in intensity [12–17]. In fact, most N-dots exhibit only blue FL emission, which greatly hinders their application in sensors, multicolor imaging, and so on. Therefore, the development of a facile method to prepare multicolor emissive N-dots (M-Ndots) continues to be necessary for promoting practical applications of N-dots.

As conventional analytical methods, carbon dots for sensing pesticides in real, complex samples can be tedious in that they require careful sample preparation steps such as extraction, clean-up and enrichment [19]. To the best of our knowledge, there are no studies focusing on the combination of sample preparation methods with M-Ndots for sensing pesticides in real complex samples. Therefore, we attempted to implement a fast and high-throughput samples preparation function into the M-Ndots sensing strategies, which would significantly expand their practical application potential.

Herein, an M-Ndots fluorescence sensing strategy equipped with a gas membrane separation device (GMSD), which was proposed in our previous work [20], was applied to in situ pesticides sensing in a real, complex sample. First, the fluorescent M-Ndots were designed by using 5-amino-1H-tetrazole (5-AT) and p-phenylenediamine (pPD) as the precursors, resulting in 31% fluorescence quantum yield. The as-synthethised M-Ndots exhibited multiple sensing signals. The M-Ndots were then applied to detect thiram and chlorpyrifos in real, complex samples by implementing a miniaturized array gas membrane separation device. As novel members of the quantum-dot family, the M-Ndots hold great promise to broaden their applications potential.

5-amino-1H-tetrazole, p-phenylenediamine acetylcholine chloride (Ach), choline oxidase (ChOx), and quinine sulphate (98%, suitable for fluorescence) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 2-aminothiazole (97%) was obtained from Shanghai Shaoyuan Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Aluminium chloride (99.99%), sodium chloride (99.5%), magnesium sulphate (99%), and zinc nitrate (98.0%) were purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory. Acetylcholinesterase (AChE) was obtained from J&K (Beijing, China). Bovine serum and phosphate buffered saline (PBS) were obtained from Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Penicillin-Streptomycin was from Sigma-Aldrich (Missouri, U.S.A.). All other chemical reagents were of analytical grade, from Shenyang Chemical Reagent Factory (Shenyang, China), and were used without any further treatment. Ultrapure water was used in all experiments.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were recorded with a Philips TECNAI 10 TEM instrument (Philips, Netherlands). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were recorded with a Dimension FastScan (Bruker, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out with a Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALab250. Microwave synthesis experiments were performed on an MWave-5000 microwave apparatus (Sineo Microwave Chemistry Technology Company, Shanghai, China). Infrared absorption spectra were acquired by a Nicolet Avatar 330 Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Nicolet). 1HNMR spectra were recorded using a Bruker AVB-400 MHz NMR spectrometer (Bruker biospin, Switzerland). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) spectra were taken in an Empyrean system. FL spectra were obtained on an RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded with a UV–Vis 2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The pH measurements were performed with a Sartorius basic pH-meter PB-10 (Sartorius, Germany). The absorbance of the cell viability by CCK-8 was measured using a microplate reader (Thermo Scientific Multiskan GO, Finland). The fluorescence spectra and images of the resulting solutions were recorded at room temperature (25 °C). LC-MS/MS for the comparison study was performed by a Shimadzu MS 8050; the parameters for the MS are presented in Table S1. The sample preparation for LC-MS/MS is shared in “Supporting Information”.

The fluorescence M-Ndots with a high percentage of elemental nitrogen were prepared in minutes by microwave-assisted hydrothermal method using 5-amino-1H-tetrazole and p-phenylenediamine as the precursors (3.0 g and 1.0 g, respectively) dissolved in deionized water (100 mL). At the reaction temperature of 140 °C, different M-Ndots were designed by varying the irradiation time from 1 to 15 min under 500 W. The solution ultimately turned greenish black and in some cases to dark brown, which suggested the formation of M-Ndots. The supernatant was collected by removing the large dots through filtration with a 0.22 μm filter after centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 15 min. For further purification, the transparent solution was dialyzed for 24 h against double distilled water using a dialysis membrane (cut off = 3500 Da). After vacuum-freeze drying, cinnamon-colored solid residue was obtained. A specific amount of obtained M-Ndots were dispersed in water and used for further studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thiram detection in fruits based on miniaturized array gas membrane separation/M-Ndots sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The sample preparation process was similar to our previous work with some modifications [20]. Firstly, the fruit samples were weighed and peeled. The skin of the samples was cut into pieces and about 0.10 g of skin was added into each tube and mixed with 200 μL of SnCl2 aqueous solution (0.20 g/mL). For use as solutions, 100 μL of thiram standard solutions and 200 μL of SnCl2 aqueous solution were directly poured into array tubes. Then, an e-PTFE membrane was affixed on each subsequent tube by adding 80 μL of aqueous solution that contained triethanolamine (10.0 mg/mL), diethylamine (4.0 mg/mL) and Cu(CH3COO)2 (20.0 μg/mL) on the top of the e-PTFE membrane for absorbing the volatile targets. The array tubes were heated at 100 °C for about 5 min. Next, 10 μL of M-Ndots (100 μg/mL) was added into the absorption solution and mixed. Finally, the mixed solution was transferred to fluorescence detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chlorpyrifos detection in vegetables based on miniaturized array gas membrane separation/M-Ndots sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the vegetables samples were crushed into pieces, then about 0.10 g of sample was added into each tube and mixed with 100 μL of aqueous solution. For use as a standard, 100 μL of chlorpyrifos standard solution was directly poured into array tubes. Then, an e-PTFE membrane was affixed on each tube following by adding 80 μL of aqueous solution that contained Fe2+ (1.0 mmol/L), citric acid (1.0 mmol/L), AchE (2.0 U/mL), and ChOx (2.0 U/mL) on the top of the e-PTFE membrane for absorbing the volatile targets. The array tubes were heated at 100 °C for about 5 min. Next, 10 μL of Ach (10.0 mmol/L) and 10 μL of M-Ndots (100 μg/mL) were added into the absorption solution and mixed. Finally, after 10 min of incubation at room temperature, the mixed solution was transferred to fluorescence detection.

Although many reports have proved that N-dots exhibited satisfactory performance [12–17], in fact, most N-dots show only blue FL emission. In recent years, the use of a multicolor emission nanosensor is a key requirement to implementing the fluorescence of quantum dots (QDs) successfully in nearly all practical applications [21–23]. Consequently, the preparation of QDs with tuneable emission has been paid considerable attention. The development of a facile method to prepare multicolor emissive N-dots (M-Ndots) is yet necessary for promoting more practical applications of N-dots. Phenylamine has been usually employed in previous studies because it enlarges conjugated π-domains easily [21,24]. The larger the size of the conjugated π-domain, the smaller the band gap, and the more redshift of the emission peak occurs [25]. However, using phenylamine as the precursor does not usually produce a high quantum yield (QY) of these materials. N-related functional groups produced by doping or surface passivation will have a considerable effect on the photoluminescence of carbon dots (CDs) [26–28]. In Lin’s research, Ndots containing a high percentage of nitrogen (about 34%) were synthesised with 5-aminotetrazole as the nitrogen source; however, the N-dots showed only blue FL emission [16]. Consequently, in order to obtain multicolor N-dots with a high QY, we attempted to introduce 5-amino-1H-tetrazole as the N-rich precursor, to cross-link with p-phenylenediamine (Fig. 1 ).

Most importantly, we investigated the effect of the weight ratio of raw materials [16,27]. When the ratio of 5-amino-1H-tetrazole to p-phenylenediamine was 4:1, the emission of N-dots was still blue, when the ratio was 2:1, the QY of M-Ndots was 25%. Fortunately, when the ratio of them was 3:1, the QY of M-Ndots was increased to 31%. Thus, the mass ratio of 3:1 was selected for M-Ndots synthesis.

To get better optical properties for the synthetic M-Ndots, we further optimized the reaction conditions [29,30]. As Table S2 results show, at the same temperature, the QY reaches a maximum up to 31% when the microwave treating time was 10 min, and it decreased with increasing time. The results suggest that the reaction was accelerated with increasing time, which may be due in part to the aggregation of the M-Ndots or the formation of more large-sized particles [15]. Therefore, the microwave heating temperature and time were set to be 140 °C and 10 min respectively for the synthesis of these M-Ndots in further characterization and applications.

According to Table S3, this method preparing for M-Ndots with high QY is facile and timesaving [12,16,31–35]. As shown in Fig. 2 a and Fig. S1, the M-Ndots exhibited unique emission spectra when excited using different excitation wavelengths (λex) in contrast to previously reported N-dots [12–17]. Following the increase of the excitation wavelength from 300 to 520 nm, the emission wavelength increased from nearly 320 to 700 nm, as in the case of most multicolor CDs. The excitation-dependent multicolor emission property may be ascribed to surface chemical functional groups [31] and surface state [32]. Specifically, four emission peaks of M-Ndots centred at 364, 420, 483, and 580 nm with broad emission peaks are observed when the sample is excited using various λex [36]. Thus, M-Ndots can emit a variety of colors if excited by different values of λex. Interestingly, for the first time it is demonstrated that N-dots can exhibit multicolor light emission properties. Furthermore, the M-Ndots showed properties of the upconversion fluorescence spectra (Fig. S2a) [14,33], wide tolerance of pH (Fig. S2b) and salt (Fig. S2c), long-time stability (Fig. S2d) and low biological toxicity (Fig. S3) [15]. Moreover, Fig. 2b shows the Commission International d’Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity coordinates of M-Ndots, which suggest that the M-Ndots we synthesised were detectable with multicolor emission spectroscopy [33].

Characterization of the surface states of M-Ndots was performed to illustrate the reason for multicolor emission spectroscopy; sp3 and sp2 hybridised carbon on the surface of the quantum dots and other surface defects can lead to multicolor emissions of their local electronic states, resulting in various functional groups [24,32]. High-resolution TEM (Fig. 3 a) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Fig. 3b) illustrated that the as-synthesised M-Ndots were well monodispersed and uniform with an average size of 5.0 nm. As shown in Fig. S4, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern presented a weak dispersed diffraction peak centred at 2θ = 27.2°, which corresponds to the value of the basal plane distance [36]. UV–Vis spectrum and FT-IR spectra confirmed the presence of various functional groups, including CO, CC, C–N, and O–H (Fig. S5). The C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s spectra shown in X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results (Fig. 3c) indicate that the M-Ndots were mainly composed of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. The N1s analysis revealed four different types of nitrogen atoms: CN, N–C3, C–N–C and NH2 (Fig. 3d), which were due to the N-rich precursors 5-AT and pPD [15]. Further elemental analysis of the M-Ndots prepared under optimal conditions indicated the nitrogen proportion (C: 23.65%, H: 5.37%, N: 47.32%), which is consistent with the XPS results. In summary, the M-Ndots were successfully synthesised by 10 min of microwave irradiation at 140 °C with 5-AT and pPD, and had a high fluorescence quantum yield at a maximum of 31%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of the sensing strategy of M-Ndots for pesticide detection
ce:section.ce:para: Traditionally, metal ions could trigger aggregation of C-dots or N-dots to form chemosensing ensembles because of the abundant functional groups on their surface, which provide an opportunity to interact with metal ions [37,38]. Thus, the as-synthesised FL responses of M-Ndots to various metal ions (Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Fe3+) were thoroughly investigated by three-dimensional fluorescence, which could reflect the situation of fluorescence quenching in different ranges of excitation and emission wavelengths [39–41]. Fig. 4 shows that the FL of the M-Ndots was quenched by different degrees with the introduction of metal ions. While the Cd2+ and Hg2+ ions nearly completely quenched the FL of M-Ndots, there was no target sample which could recover the FL intensity. The quenching regions of the metal ions of Cu2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, and Fe3+ were similar, resulting in the same shape of three-dimensional fluorescence spectra. Thus, considering the ability of the M-Ndots to quench metal ions, the M-Ndots could act as a “bridge” between quantum dots and analytes. An application of M-Ndots was therefore designed to sense thiram and chlorpyrifos by employing Cu2+ and Fe3+ [40,41]. Furthermore, we attempted to combine the process with gas membrane separation, which was designed to eliminate influence from the matrix and to improve the reproducibility of the detection signal during real sample analysis. Therefore, the influence of the non-volatile ions such as Cu2+ and Fe3+, which might be in real samples, was eliminated. The processes of sensing thiram and chlorpyrifos with M-Ndots are shown in Fig. 5 a and Fig. 5b, respectively.

As one type of dithiocarbamate residue, thiram can be decomposed into CS2 by an oxidation-reduction reaction with SnCl2 in acidic and heating conditions in a miniaturized array GMSD tube of array miniaturized GMSD [20,40]. CS2 could penetrate the membrane of the GMSD and be absorbed by and react with aqueous solution on the membrane. The membrane contains triethanolamine, diethylamine, and Cu(CH3COO)2, resulting in the product of bis(diethyldithiocarbamate)copper(II) [41]. Here, the Cu2+ was clearly consumed and the FL of the M-Ndots was “turned on” (Fig. 5a). The feasibility of this strategy is demonstrated in Fig. 6 a. It could be seen that the FL of M-Ndots was “turned off” by Cu2+ and then was “turned on” in the presence of thiram, which was treated with the GMSD. Chlorpyrifos has a boiling point in the range of 41.5–44 °C [42], which is easily volatilised by the heating of the GMSD. The volatilised chlorpyrifos could then be reabsorbed by the aqueous solution, which contained the enzyme of AchE. The activity of AchE was inhibited by chlorpyrifos so that the process of Fe2+ oxidization to Fe3+ was influenced, which prevented the FL of M-Ndots from being quenched by Fe3+ (Fig. 5b). The feasibility of this strategy is displayed in Fig. 6c. It could be seen that the FL of M-Ndots was “turned off” by Fe3+ in the absence of chlorpyrifos. However, when the chlorpyrifos existed, the FL quenching of M-Ndots was inhibited.

In the optimized conditions, the linear ranges for thiram were 0.5–2.5 μg/mL (R2 = 0.9993) with detection limits of 0.1 μg/mL (Fig. 6b). The linear ranges for chlorpyrifos were 0.01–0.50 μg/mL (R2 = 0.9932) with detection limits of 0.002 μg/mL (Fig. 6d). The sensitivity meets the requirements of national standard method (GB/T 2763–2016). The feasibility and duplicability were investigated via the recovery of spiked samples and intra/inter-day RSDs, respectively. The results are illustrated in Table 1 . The recoveries of thiram in apple, longan and grape samples ranged from 90.0 to 113.0%, while chlorpyrifos in cole, lettuce and cucumber ranged from 90.0 to 115.0%. The intra-day RSDs of thiram and chlorpyrifos were 4.0–7.7% and 4.0–7.8%, respectively, while the inter-day RSDs of thiram and chlorpyrifos were 5.0–9.7% and 5.5–10.0%, respectively. This suggests that the M-Ndots sensing method was feasible and reproducible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application of M-Ndots in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate their practical applications, the M-Ndots were applied to detecting thiram and chlorpyrifos in real samples after the optimization of sensing thiram and chlorpyrifos (Fig. S6 and Fig. S7) [43,44]. Fig. S8 shows that the primary matrices including glucose and organic acid (Table S4) in the fruits and vegetables samples did not affect the M-Ndots’ sensing abilities after treatment with the GMSD.

Based on these results, it was believed that the volatile species in the real samples could not influence the detection results due to the following reasons. Firstly, the temperature during the sample preparation was relatively low enough (100 °C) that most of the high boiling point species could not volatilised. Moreover, the sample processing time was short (5 min). Lastly, the detection strategy of M-Ndots was selective. For example, the derivative reaction was selective to CS2 and the AChE was selective to OPs.

Fig.S9 illustrated the feasibility of M-Ndots for sensing thiram and chlorpyrifos in real samples, which was further confirmed by LC-MS/MS (Fig. S10). The practical application results are presented in Table 2 . M-Ndots were employed for sensing thiram in fruits samples such as lychee, pear, and orange. The results for thiram ranged from 1.21 mg/kg to 4.63 mg/kg with RSDs of 4.2–7.8%. The relative error in the LC-MS/MS results ranged from −7.4% to +8.1%. M-Ndots were also employed for sensing chlorpyrifos in vegetable samples such as cole, lettuce and cucumber. The amounts of chlorpyrifos ranged from 0.24 μg/kg to 0.80 μg/kg with RSDs of 4.0–7.2%. The relative error in the LC-MS/MS results ranged from −7.7% to +10.1%. These results suggest that M-Ndots coupled with a gas membrane separation was successful for the rapid detection of pesticides in fruit and vegetable samples.

A comparison of M-Ndots to other QDs applied to the fluorescence detection of pesticides is shown in Table 3 [9,45–50]. The proposed method based on M-Ndots had satisfied the criteria for sensitivity and detection range. However, the key to realising the application of QDs for sensing was to improve its selectivity; because matrix interferences commonly occur in trace analysis, which may lead to inaccurate results, particularly in complex matrices. The strategies for improving the selectivity of QD-based nanosensors were presented in detail in the review of Prof. Yang’s research in details [3], These mainly included immobilization of biological materials (using enzymes) or chemical materials (molecularly imprinted polymer) as recognition receptors, or QDs as signal transducers and amplifiers. It could be deduced from the objective of Prof. Yang’s research that cleaning up matrices was a key factor in improving the selectivity of QDs sensors.

In this vein, the advantages of the proposed method were clear with multi-sample preparation integration for improving selectivity, including using enzymes (such as AChE), derivatization, and the GMSD. To benefit from this, the M-Ndots combined with the GMSD could be applied in a complex matrix a “turn on” model instead of a “turn off” model for a simple matrix, such as water. However, the strategy of using M-Ndots combined with the GMSD was not selective for other kinds of dithiocarbamates or organophosphates because the dithiocarbamates could be decomposed into CS2 and the organophosphates could inhibit AChE. Despite this, when comparing the presented method to other analytical methods for detection of dithiocarbamates or organophosphates pesticides, such as GC-MS, UV–vis, SERS, and LC-MS/MS (Table S5), the obvious merit of our proposed method is its short operation time consuming. The entire process of sample preparation and detection just took 5 min.

In summary, multicolor nitrogen quantum dots were synthesised by a microwave-assisted method using 5-amino-1H-tetrazole and p-phenylenediamine as precursors. The maximum emission of the M-Ndots gradually shifted from blue to red light, and they exhibited a high fluorescence quantum yield. The M-Ndots were successfully used as a sensor for detection of thiram and chlorpyrifos, based on the complexation reaction between the M-Ndots and either Cu2+ or Fe3+. Taking advantage of the excellent optical property of M-Ndots and the pre-treatment of miniaturized array gas membrane separation, the fluorometric platform is a promising candidate for rapid detection of pesticides. We expect that the findings of this study will contribute to future experiments focused on the synthesis of fluorescent nanomaterials with diverse colors and to research on the interaction between pesticides and nanomaterials.
The author(s) declare that they have no competing interest.

Zhijiao Tang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Investigation, Writing - original draft, preparation. Zhengyi Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Investigation, Writing - original draft, preparation. Gongke Li: Visualization, Resources, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Project administration, Writing - review & editing. Yuling Hu: Visualization, Resources, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Project administration, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2019YFC1606101), the Research and Development Plan for Key Areas of Food Safety in Guangdong Province of China (No. 2019B020211001), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21675178 and 21976213), respectively.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2020.08.038.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Double-decrease of the fluorescence of CdSe/ZnS quantum dots for the detection of zinc(II) dimethyldithiocarbamate (ziram) based on its interaction with gold nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Yang, X. Zhang, J. Wang, H. Sun, L. Jiang, Double-decrease of the fluorescence of CdSe/ZnS quantum dots for the detection of zinc(II) dimethyldithiocarbamate (ziram) based on its interaction with gold nanoparticles, Microchim Acta 185 (2018) 472.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Impaired pesticide removal and detoxification by biomixtures during the simulated pesticide application cycle of a tropical agricultural system

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Acosta-Sanchez, C. Soto-Garita, M. Masis-Mora, J.C. Cambronero-Heinrichs, C.E. Rodriguez-Rodriguez, Impaired pesticide removal and detoxification by biomixtures during the simulated pesticide application cycle of a tropical agricultural system, Ecotox. Environ. Safe 195 (2020) 110460.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence detection of pesticides using quantum dot materials-A review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.A. Nsibande, P.B.C. Forbes, Fluorescence detection of pesticides using quantum dot materials-A review, Anal. Chim. Acta 945 (2016) 9-22.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Technological advancement in electrochemical biosensor based detection of Organophosphate pesticide chlorpyrifos in the environment: a review of status and prospects

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Uniyal, R.K. Sharma, Technological advancement in electrochemical biosensor based detection of Organophosphate pesticide chlorpyrifos in the environment: A review of status and prospects, Biosens. Bioelectron. 116 (2018) 37-50.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Charuaud, E. Jarde, A. Jaffrezic, M. Thomas, B. Le Bot, Veterinary pharmaceutical residues from natural water to tap water: Sales, occurrence and fate, J. Hazard. Mater. 361 (2019) 169-186.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Lin, X. Lin, S. Han, Y. Liu, W. Hasi, L. Wang, Flexible fabrication of a paper-fluidic SERS sensor coated with a monolayer of core-shell nanospheres for reliable quantitative SERS measurements, Anal. Chim. Acta 1108 (2020) 167-176.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Chen, Y. Huang, P. Kannan, L. Zhang, Z. Lin, J. Zhang, T. Chen, L. Guo, Flexible and adhesive surface enhance raman scattering active tape for rapid detection of pesticide residues in fruits and vegetables, Anal. Chem. 88 (2016) 2149-2155.

Sun, Monitoring of organophosphorus pesticides in vegetables using monoclonal antibody-based direct competitive ELISA followed by HPLC-MS/MS, Food Chem. 131 (2012) 1569-1576.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Lin, Y. Yan, M. Guo, Y. Cao, Y. Yu, T. Zhang, Y. Huang, D. Wu, Modification-free carbon dots as turn-on fluorescence probe for detection of organophosphorus pesticides, Food Chem. 245 (2018) 1176-1182.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhu, Y. Song, X. Zhao, J. Shao, J. Zhang, B. Yang, The photoluminescence mechanism in carbon dots (graphene quantum dots, carbon nanodots, and polymer dots): current state and future perspective, Nano Res. 8 (2015) 355-381.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhang, J. Zhao, X. Sun, W. Pan, G. Yu, J. Wang, Fluorescent carbon dots for probing the effect of thiram on the membrane of fungal cell and its quantitative detection in aqueous solution, Sensor and Actuat B: Chem. 273 (2018) 1833-1842.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Tang, Z. Lin, G. Li, Y. Hu, Amino nitrogen quantum dots-based nanoprobe for fluorescence detection and imaging of cysteine in biological samples, Anal. Chem. 89 (2017) 4238-4245.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Tang, J. Yang, G. Li, Y. Hu, Synthesis of sulfur-rich nitrogen dots from a single source precursor and its application in dual-mode sensing, Talanta 195 (2019) 550-557.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Lin, J. Yang, Z. Tang, G. Li, Y. Hu, Nitrogen dots as reductant and stabilizer for the synthesis of AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites for efficient surface-enhanced Raman scattering detection, Talanta 178 (2018) 515-521.

Chen, Red emission B, N, S-co-doped carbon dots for colorimetric and fluorescent dual mode detection of Fe3+ ions in complex biological fluids and living cells, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 9 (2017) 12663-12672.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Yan, Z. Sun, H. Zhang, X. Sun, Y. Jiang, Z. Bai, The fluorescence mechanism of carbon dots, and methods for tuning their emission color: a review, Microchim Acta 186 (2019) 583.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Amin, A. Afkhami, L. Hosseinzadeh, T. Madrakian, Green and cost-effective synthesis of carbon dots from date kernel and their application as a novel switchable fluorescence probe for sensitive assay of Zoledronic acid drug in human serum and cellular imaging, Anal. Chim. Acta 1030 (2018) 183-193.
